Name: informationtheoreticpruningbounds
Title: Information-Theoretic Pruning Bounds for Deep Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the optimal pruning strategy for deep neural networks can be derived from an information-theoretic framework, specifically by leveraging rate-distortion theory to balance the trade-off between model compression and task performance. This approach will provide a principled way to allocate bits to different layers and neurons, ensuring that the compressed model maintains high predictive sufficiency while minimizing redundancy.
Related Work: Previous works, such as 'An Information-Theoretic Justification for Model Pruning' by Isik et al. (2021) and 'Information-Theoretic Understanding of Population Risk Improvement with Model Compression' by Bu et al. (2019), have explored the use of information theory in model compression. However, these works primarily focus on the theoretical bounds and empirical validation of pruning strategies. Our proposal distinguishes itself by providing a novel, information-theoretic framework that not only justifies pruning but also derives specific pruning policies based on the rate-distortion trade-off. This approach aims to achieve a more fine-grained and adaptive pruning strategy that can be applied across different layers and neurons.
Abstract: We propose an information-theoretic framework for deriving optimal pruning policies in deep neural networks. By framing pruning as a rate-distortion problem, we aim to balance the trade-off between model compression and task performance. Our approach leverages mutual information and entropy to quantify the information content of each neuron and layer, and uses this information to derive a principled way to allocate bits. We hypothesize that this method will provide a more adaptive and fine-grained pruning strategy, leading to compressed models that maintain high predictive sufficiency while minimizing redundancy. We validate our approach through experiments on popular datasets, comparing the performance of our pruning policies against existing methods.
Experiments: 1. **Entropy Estimation**: Estimate the entropy of the activations in each layer of a pre-trained neural network using mutual information lower bounds (e.g., InfoNCE). This will provide a measure of the information content of each layer.

2. **Rate-Distortion Curves**: Derive the rate-distortion curves for each layer by varying the pruning threshold and measuring the resulting distortion in task performance. Use these curves to identify the optimal pruning threshold for each layer.

3. **Pruning Policy Derivation**: Derive a pruning policy that allocates bits to each layer based on the rate-distortion trade-off. Specifically, layers with higher information content (lower entropy) will be allocated more bits, while layers with lower information content will be pruned more aggressively.

4. **Experimental Validation**: Apply the derived pruning policy to a pre-trained neural network and evaluate its performance on a standard dataset (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet). Compare the performance of the pruned model with the original model and with models pruned using existing methods (e.g., magnitude-based pruning, L1-norm pruning).

5. **Robustness Analysis**: Test the robustness of the pruned model to distributional shifts and noisy inputs by evaluating its performance on adversarial examples and out-of-distribution data. Compare the robustness of the pruned model with that of the original model and existing pruning methods.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. **Estimation Accuracy**: The accuracy of the entropy and mutual information estimators can significantly affect the derived pruning policies. We will need to carefully validate the estimators using synthetic data and theoretical bounds.

2. **Computational Complexity**: The computational cost of estimating mutual information and deriving rate-distortion curves can be high, especially for large models. We will need to ensure that our methods are computationally feasible and can be applied within the resource constraints of an academic lab.

3. **Generalization to Different Architectures**: Our approach may not generalize well to different neural network architectures and datasets. We will need to validate our methods on a diverse set of models and datasets to ensure robustness.

4. **Sensitivity to Hyperparameters**: The derived pruning policies may be sensitive to the choice of hyperparameters, such as the pruning threshold and the mutual information estimator. We will need to perform a thorough hyperparameter tuning and sensitivity analysis to ensure robustness.
